'''Tsukimeka''' (''tsukimeka''; Tsukimeka-ō) was a {{ill|imperial family|ja|皇族|lt=member of the imperial family|lt_ja=皇族}} of ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who appears in the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}''.

He was the great-grandson (third-generation descendant) of the 15th {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, and is considered the founding ancestor of the {{ill|Tsukushi-Meta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|筑志米多国造}}. There is no mention of him in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' or the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}''.

== Genealogy ==
According to the "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}" of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', he was the grandson of {{ill|Waka-nuke-futamata-no-Mikoto|ja|稚野毛二派皇子|lt=Waka-numake-futamata-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=稚沼毛二俣命}} (''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'': Waka-nuke-futamata-no-Miko; ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'': Waka-nuke-futamata-ō), the imperial {{ill|prince|ja|皇子}} of the 15th {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, making him the great-grandson (third-generation descendant) of Emperor Ōjin{{Sfn|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}.

== Tomb ==
{{Double image stack|right|Uenobyuuzuka-kofun Tsukimekaou-bo.JPG|Uenobyuuzuka-kofun Tsukimekaou-bo baichou.JPG|200|{{center|Top: Tsukimeka-ō Tomb worship site<br />Bottom: Model of other relocated kofun<br />({{ill|Kamimine, Saga|ja|上峰町|lt=Kamimine|lt_ja=上峰町}}, {{ill|Miyaki District|ja|三養基郡}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}})}}}}
The {{ill|imperial mausoleum|ja|陵墓|lt=tomb|lt_ja=墓}} is designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as '''Tsukimeka-ō Tomb''' (''tsukimeka no miko no haka''; {{ウィキ座標|33|19|49.63|N|130|25|8.47|E|region:JP-41|位置|name=Tsukimeka-ō Tomb (Kami-no-byūzuka Kofun)}}), located in Bōjo, {{ill|Kamimine, Saga|ja|上峰町|lt=Kamimine|lt_ja=上峰町}}, {{ill|Miyaki District|ja|三養基郡}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}{{Sfn|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}<ref>''Kunaichō Shoryōbu Ryōbo Chikei-zu Shūsei'', Gakuseisha, 1999, "Table of All Successive Imperial Tombs" at the end of the volume.</ref>. The archaeological site name is "Kami-no-byūzuka Kofun" (''kami-no-byūzuka kofun'')<ref>The "Kami-no-byū" in "Kami-no-byūzuka Kofun" is a place name.</ref>.

The kofun is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} with its rectangular front facing west{{Sfn|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}. The mound is 49 meters long and is estimated to have been built in the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, but the burial facility is unclear{{Sfn|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}. {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} and cobblestone facing have been found on the surface of the mound, and it has been confirmed that a moat surrounded the mound{{Sfn|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}. In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 18 ([[1943]]), the Ministry of the Imperial Household (now the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) designated it as the tomb of Tsukimeka-ō{{Sfn|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}.

In the surrounding hill area, the {{ill|Metabaru Kofun Cluster|ja|目達原古墳群|lt=Metabaru Kofun Cluster|lt_ja=目達原古墳群}} (''metabaru kofungun'') once existed, including Ōtsuka Kofun, Furuinari-zuka Kofun, Inari-zuka Kofun, Tsukayama Kofun, Hyōtan-zuka Kofun, Mumei-zuka Kofun, Yokota-minami Kofun, and others{{Sfn|目達原古墳群（平凡社）|1980年}}. However, they were destroyed during the construction of Metabaru Airfield ({{ill|JGSDF Metabaru Garrison|ja|目達原駐屯地|lt=Metabaru Garrison|lt_ja=目達原駐屯地}}) in Shōwa 17–18 ([[1942]]–[[1943]]), and were reinterred in a model mound built on the north side of Kami-no-byūzuka Kofun{{Sfn|目達原古墳群（平凡社）|1980年}}{{Sfn|目達原古墳群（国史）}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Yoshinogari Site|ja|吉野ヶ里遺跡}}.

== Descendant Kuni no Miyatsuko ==
The "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}" of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' lists the following {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} as descendants:
* {{ill|Tsukushi-Meta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|筑志米多国造|lt=Tsukushi-Meta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt_ja=竺志米多国造}}
*: During the reign of Shiga Takaanaho-chō (the 13th {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}), Tsukimeka, grandson of {{ill|Waka-nuke-futamata-no-Mikoto|ja|稚野毛二派皇子|lt=Waka-numake-futamata-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=稚沼毛二俣命}} — a common ancestor with the {{ill|Okinaga clan|ja|息長氏|lt=Okinaga-no-Kimi|lt_ja=息長公}} — was appointed kuni no miyatsuko.
*: This is estimated to be in the Meta-gō area of {{ill|Mine District|ja|三根郡}} of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} (the area around present-day {{ill|Kamimine, Saga|ja|上峰町}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}}), or in the Mata-gō area of {{ill|Yasu District|ja|夜須郡}} of {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国}} / the Mata-gō area of {{ill|Shimotsuza District|ja|下座郡|lt=Shimoza District|lt_ja=下坐郡}} (the area around present-day {{ill|Asakura, Fukuoka|ja|朝倉市}} in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}})<ref name="国造">''Kokuzō-sei no Kenkyū: Shiryō-hen / Ronkō-hen'' (Yagi Shoten, 2013), pp. 276-277.</ref>. Because Tsukimeka was a cousin of {{ill|Emperor Ankō|ja|安康天皇}} (20th) and {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} (21st), "Shiga Takaanaho-chō" is presumed to refer to Emperor Ankō (whose Japanese-style posthumous name was Anaho-no-Sumera-Mikoto){{Sfn|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', {{ill|Oho-hodo-ō|ja|意富富杼王}}, son of Waka-nuke-futamata-ō (Waka-nuke-futamata-no-Miko), is recorded as the ancestor of "Tsukushi-no-Mata-no-Kimi" and others<ref name="国造"/>.

== Footnotes ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}}
{{reflist}}

== References ==
* {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=斎藤忠|authorlink=斎藤忠|year=|chapter=目達原古墳群|title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|目達原古墳群（国史）}}}}
* {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1980|chapter=|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 42 佐賀県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=|ref=4582490425}}
** {{Wikicite|reference=「目達原古墳群」|ref={{Harvid|目達原古墳群（平凡社）|1980年}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=「都紀女加王墓」|ref={{Harvid|都紀女加王墓（平凡社）|1980年}}}}

== See also ==
{{Commonscat|Kaminobyuuzuka-kofun|Kami-no-byūzuka Kofun}}
* {{ill|Tsukushi-Meta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|筑志米多国造}}
* {{ill|Meta-no-Hokusuke|ja|米多北助}}
* {{ill|List of Figures from before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}
{{リダイレクトの所属カテゴリ
|redirect1=上のびゅう塚古墳
|1-1=佐賀県の古墳
|1-2=上峰町
|1-3=前方後円墳
|1-4=日本の陵墓
}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:つきめか}}
[[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]]
[[Category:古墳時代の人物]]
[[Category:古代日本の人物]]
[[Category:肥前国の人物]]
[[Category:生没年不詳]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:都紀女加]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
